coredata.dc:title: Formation of homogeneous pyridinic emission states in carbon dots via solid-state carbonization of aminoquinoline: Optical design principles and light-emitting diode applications
coredata.dc:creator: Byun, Yerim
Lee, Dajin
Bae, Sang Woo
Won, Sungyeon
Cho, Soohyun
Lee, Su Hwan
Park, Yoonsang
Kim, Young-Hoon
Kwon, Woosung
coredata.dc:description: Developing carbon dots (CDs) for practical applications faces significant hurdles due to non-homogeneity in their chemical structure, leading to broad spectral distribution and the excitation-energy-dependent behavior of their fluorescence (FL) emission properties. To address this challenge, we implemented solid-state carbonization of 6-aminoquinoline (6-AQ) devoid of any additives that could induce chemical non-homogeneity. This process yielded pyridinic-nitrogen-doped CDs (PN-CDs) characterized by homogenous, uniform emission states arising from pyridine-incorporated heteroatomic structures. Notably, these PN-CDs exhibited excitation-energy-independent FL at 520 nm with a full-width-half-maximum of 70 nm. To elucidate the structure-property relationship in PN-CDs, a comprehensive analysis encompassing time-resolved spectroscopy and ab initio calculations was conducted. Furthermore, we performed a comparative study by manipulating synthesis parameters and utilizing six AQ isomers, offering valuable insights to optimize the optical properties of PN-CDs. Finally, we successfully demonstrated high-color-purity and efficient green light-emitting diodes utilizing PN-CDs, highlighting their potential for future display applications. This paves the way for advancement in optical materials with enhanced performance and reduced environmental impact.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Pyridine
Aminoquinoline
Green
Light-emitting diodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Basic Science Programs (NRF-2022R1A2C4002403 and NRF-2022R1C1C1008282) and the Engineering Research Center Program (NRF-2022R1A5A1032539) funded by the National Research Foundation of Korea.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of PN-CD-200. (b) Photos of PN-CD-200 in toluene (1 mg mL−1) under daylight (left) and UV (365 nm) irradiation. (c) DLS size distribution of PN-CD-200. (d) Raman spectrum of PN-CD-200. (e) XRD pattern of PN-CD-200. (f) FTIR spectra of 6-AQ and PN-CD-200.
(a) 1H NMR spectra of 6-AQ and PN-CD-200. (b, c, d) High-resolution XPS (b) carbon 1 s, (c) nitrogen 1 s, and (d) oxygen 1 s spectra of PN-CD-200.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of 6-AQ and PN-CD-200 and the FL excitation spectrum of PN-CD-200. (b) FL emission contour map of PN-CD-200. (c) FL emission spectra of PN-CD-200. (d) Tauc plot of PN-CD-200. (e) TRPL spectra of PN-CD-200. (f, g) Molecular orbital energy levels and band structures of (f) the pyranthrene base and (g) pyranthrene containing four pyridine units (gray: carbon, blue: nitrogen, and white: hydrogen). EX and EM designate excitation and emission, respectively.

(a) Schematic of PN-CD-200 LED architecture. (b) Luminance–voltage (black) and current density–voltage (red) characteristics of PN-CD-200 LEDs. (c) Current efficiency, (d) power efficiency, (e) EQE, and (f) EL spectrum (applied bias = 4.6 V) of PN-CD-200 LEDs.
Schematic of the PN-CD-200 synthesis pathway from 6-AQ through the benzenoid ring destruction and nucleophilic carboxylation.

head.ce:title: Formation of homogeneous pyridinic emission states in carbon dots via solid-state carbonization of aminoquinoline: Optical design principles and light-emitting diode applications

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing carbon dots (CDs) for practical applications faces significant hurdles due to non-homogeneity in their chemical structure, leading to broad spectral distribution and the excitation-energy-dependent behavior of their fluorescence (FL) emission properties. To address this challenge, we implemented solid-state carbonization of 6-aminoquinoline (6-AQ) devoid of any additives that could induce chemical non-homogeneity. This process yielded pyridinic-nitrogen-doped CDs (PN-CDs) characterized by homogenous, uniform emission states arising from pyridine-incorporated heteroatomic structures. Notably, these PN-CDs exhibited excitation-energy-independent FL at 520 nm with a full-width-half-maximum of 70 nm. To elucidate the structure-property relationship in PN-CDs, a comprehensive analysis encompassing time-resolved spectroscopy and ab initio calculations was conducted. Furthermore, we performed a comparative study by manipulating synthesis parameters and utilizing six AQ isomers, offering valuable insights to optimize the optical properties of PN-CDs. Finally, we successfully demonstrated high-color-purity and efficient green light-emitting diodes utilizing PN-CDs, highlighting their potential for future display applications. This paves the way for advancement in optical materials with enhanced performance and reduced environmental impact.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention because of their remarkable optical characteristics, biocompatibility, and chemical stability [1–4], which renders them highly desirable for a wide array of applications in fields such as bioimaging, sensing, therapeutics, and optoelectronics [5–9]. Researchers have explored methods to enhance their optical properties to broaden the scope of applications [10–13]. These methods focus on modifying CD chemical structures by introducing heteroatomic impurities such as boron, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur; [14–20] these impurities create intragap states within CDs, which lead to alterations in their electronic band structures and shifts in their photoluminescence wavelengths [21–23]. Among these impurities, nitrogen is an optimal dopant for CDs because of its comparable atomic size to carbon [24,25], and its ability to form robust covalent bonds with it. This bond formation facilitates the introduction of additional photo-excitable electrons into the ground state [26–29].

Thus far, research studies have established effective and efficient methods to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs using different precursor materials, among which, quinoline derivatives appear to be exceptionally promising candidates [30–33]. Aminoquinoline (AQ) [34], a heterocyclic compound encompassing aromatic carbon, pyridinic nitrogen, and amino nitrogen [35–37], has attained significant interest for its potential in producing high-quality nitrogen-doped CDs with unique attributes [15,16,38,39]. As a precursor material, AQ offers several key advantages:
1)
The carbon-rich framework of AQ serves as an abundant source for CD formation, thereby supplying the necessary carbon atoms during synthesis.
2)
The pyridinic substructure of AQ exhibits robust thermal stability, which persists through the carbonization process and guarantees the uniform incorporation of nitrogen in form of pyridine into the resulting CDs.
3)
The amino functional groups of AQ facilitate not only its intermolecular fusion, but also ensure surface functionalization of the resulting CDs, which enhances their functionality in terms of electronic and optical properties [40].

However, non-homogeneity within the chemical structures of CDs remains a major obstacle to their practical applications, as it leads to a broad spectral distribution and excitation-energy-dependent behavior of their FL. To overcome this challenge, we demonstrated additive-free solid-state carbonization of 6-AQ. The thermal oxidation and intermolecular fusion of 6-AQ during carbonization generates optically active pyridine-incorporated heteroatomic structures within the carbon network, yielding pyridinic-nitrogen-doped CDs (PN-CDs). These PN-CDs exhibit homogeneous, uniform emission states, resulting in excitation-energy-independent FL at 520 nm with a narrow full-width-half-maximum (FWHM) of 70 nm. To elucidate the structure-property relationship in PN-CDs, a comprehensive analysis utilizing time-resolved spectroscopy and ab initio calculations was conducted. Furthermore, we performed a comprehensive comparative analysis by manipulating the synthesis temperature and employing six AQ isomers to extract valuable insights to help formulate design principles to optimize optical properties of PN-CDs. Finally, we successfully fabricated green light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with high color purity and efficiency using these PN-CDs, thereby demonstrating their potential for future display applications.

AQ isomers (2-, 3-, 4-, 5-, 6-, 7-, and 8-AQ) were purchased from Merck. Toluene (anhydrous, 99.8%) was purchased from Samchun Chemicals. All the chemicals were used as received, without further purification or modification. Cellulose ester dialysis tubes were purchased from Spectra/Par Biotech. The representative PN-CD, denoted as PN-CD-200 (where 200 indicates the reaction temperature), was prepared as follows:
1)
6-AQ (800 mg) is heated to 200 ℃ for ∼24 h in a muffle furnace.
2)
The obtained sticky solid product is dissolved in toluene and dialyzed against toluene for at least three days using a dialysis bag with a cut-off molecular weight of 100–500 Da.
3)
Toluene was removed by rotary evaporation, and the remaining solid was re-dispersed in toluene (1 mg mL−1) and stored in an argon-purged desiccator until measurements were performed.

Three additional reaction temperatures (100 °C, 150 °C, and 300 °C) were selected to investigate the effect of synthesis temperature, while maintaining other parameters constant. The resulting products were denoted as PN-CD-100, PN-CD-150, and PN-CD-300, respectively. Additionally, 6-AQ was replaced with an equivalent molar amount of 2-, 3-, 4-, 5-, 7-, or 8-AQ in the experiments to investigate the influence of amine position, again with all other conditions unchanged.

PN-CDs were dispersed in toluene by sonication and dropped onto a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) grid for TEM observations. TEM was performed using a JEM-2200FS instrument (JEOL) equipped with a Cs corrector. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed using a Horiba SZ-100 particle size analyzer, and the 1 H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra of 6-AQ and PN-CDs dissolved in toluene-d8 were recorded using a Bruker Avance III HD500 spectrometer (500 MHz). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer, and Raman spectroscopy was conducted on an Alvatek XperRam S spectrometer upon excitation by a 532-nm laser. X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a VG Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer equipped with an A1 X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer equipped with an attenuated total reflection accessory.

PN-CD solutions were dispersed in distilled water by sonication for all spectroscopic measurements. UV–visible absorption and FL spectroscopy of the PN-CDs were performed using a Scinco S-3100 spectrophotometer and Jasco FP-8500 fluorometer with QS-grade quartz cuvettes (111-QS, Hellma Analytics, dimensions 10 mm × 10 mm), respectively. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) measurements were performed using a spectrometer coupled to a streak camera (C10910, Hamamatsu). The extended Hückel calculations were conducted using YAeHMOP software for the pyranthrene variants. Absolute quantum yield (QY) measurements were acquired using a Jasco FP-8500 fluorometer with a 100-mm integrating sphere setup (ILF-835), and they were subsequently analyzed utilizing the Jasco Spectra Manager II software.

ITO-patterned glass was sonicated in acetone and 2-isopropanol for 15 min each, and then, it was boiled in 2-isopropanol. After cleaning, it was UV-ozone treated for 20 min to make the ITO surface hydrophilic and remove the residue. After these steps, a hole-injection layer, incorporating tetra-fluoroethylene-perfluoro-3,6-dioxa-4-methyl-7-octande-sulfonic acid copolymer (PFI), and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS), was baked for 30 min at 150 °C. Subsequently, the solution containing PN-CD-200, 4,4’-bis(carbazole-9-yl)biphyenyl (CBP), and 1,3,5-tris(N-phenylbenzimidizol-2-yl)benzene (TPBI) in a tetrahydrofuran solvent was spin-coated on top of the GraHIL film in a N2 glovebox to obtain the ∼50-nm-thick emitting layer (EML). Then, electrons transporting TPBI (50 nm), LiF, and aluminum (100 nm) were sequentially deposited in a thermal evaporator under high vacuum (<10−7 Torr). The current–voltage–luminance characteristics were measured using a Keithley 2400 source measurement unit and a Minolta CS2000 spectroradiometer.

Major products derived from the thermal or chemical oxidation of 6-AQ include carboxylated pyridine compounds like quinolinic acid or cinchomernoic acid [41]. Likewise, under these reaction conditions, the benzenoid ring of 6-AQ partially undergoes oxidation and destruction, thus forming carboxylic acid moieties. The partially positive carbon attached to the amino group in 6-AQ acts as a crucial nucleophile, which facilitates carboxylation via nucleophilic attack by oxygen. The resulting carboxylic acid groups play a pivotal role in the formation of a conjugated carbon network in the resulting PN-CD-200, via decarboxylation reactions between either carboxylic acid groups or between carboxylic acid and amine groups. Scheme 1 suggests a proposed mechanism that clarifies the intricate steps involved in the synthesis of PN-CD-200.

The morphologies of the as-prepared PN-CD-200 are assessed using TEM, and the results reveal a quasi-spherical structure with sizes ranging from 10 to 50 nm ( Fig. 1a and Fig. S1). Our PN-CD-200 exhibited complete dissolution in toluene, which emits a strong and distinct green FL in the solution (Fig. 1b). The DLS measurements indicated that PN-CD-200 exhibited a size distribution ranging from 10 to 40 nm (Fig. 1c), in agreement with the TEM images that predominantly showed nanoparticles with sizes within this range. The Raman spectra of PN-CD-200 (Fig. 1d and Fig. S2) displayed the characteristic signals of partially graphitic carbon, indicating the presence of sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon blend. PN-CD-200 exhibited distinctive peaks at 1355.42 and 1542.00 cm−1, which correspond to the D and G bands, respectively, of partially graphitic carbon [42,43]. The calculated D/G ratio of ∼0.965 suggested the potential presence of a substantial amount of oxygen in PN-CD-200 (Table S1). The polycrystalline nature of PN-CD-200 was validated by XRD analysis (Fig. 1e and Fig. S3). The distinct and sharp carbon (002) diffraction band (2θ = 15–30°) indicated the predominance of disordered graphitic carbon structures in PN-CD-200. The crystallite size of carbon (002) (d002) was calculated to be ∼1.7–1.8 nm (2θ1 = 17–18°) [44,45]. Based on these findings, we deduced that PN-CD-200 has a turbostratic structure, with polyaromatic sub-structures randomly dispersed within an amorphous carbon matrix.

FTIR spectroscopy was employed to examine alterations in chemical structures pre- and post-reaction. Fig. 1f and Fig. S4 show that the spectra display diminished amine NH stretching signals of 6-AQ (1640 and 3300–3400 cm−1) alongside the emergence of carboxylic acid OH stretching (2960 and 2924 cm−1), amine salt NH stretching (2853 cm−1), and azide stretching (2160 cm−1) [46]. This indicates that the consumption of the amine functional group in 6-AQ via the oxidative destruction of the benzenoid ring followed by carboxyl- and amine-mediated fusion, forming nitrogen-containing structures like amine salts or azides. Moreover, the involvement of oxygen during the synthesis leads to the appearance of oxygen-related bonding.

The 1H NMR spectroscopic analysis of PN-CD-200 unveiled the carbonaceous core structures formed via carboxyl- and amine-mediated fusion ( Fig. 2a and Fig. S5). The pre- and post-reaction spectra indicated the reduction in amine and 2-carbon proton signals of 6-AQ at δ = 6.0–7.0 ppm, and the appearance of aromatic or sp2 carbon proton signals at δ = 8.0–8.5 ppm [47,48]. These shifts suggest the destruction of the benzenoid ring of 6-AQ and the formation of carbon cores with polyaromatic domains, respectively. Therefore, our proposed mechanism involved first the partial oxidative destruction of 6-AQ (carboxylation of the benzenoid ring), followed by decarboxylation or amidation, and carbonization, ultimately resulting in the synthesis of PN-CD-200.

XPS measurements revealed that oxygen incorporated into PN-CD-200 during the reaction period (Fig. 2b-d and Fig. S6). In the carbon 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2b), the characteristic C−C/CC peak at 284.8 eV indicates the successful carbonization of 6-AQ, which results in the formation of polycrystalline carbon cores. The nitrogen 1 s spectrum displays a pyridinic nitrogen peak at 399 eV and a C-NH2 at 401 eV, which suggests the presence of pyridinic structures in PN-CD-200 [49–51], likely originating from 6-AQ during the synthesis (Fig. 2c). The oxygen 1 s spectra exhibits peaks at 533 eV for carbonyl CO and 531 eV for C−O, further suggesting oxygen-containing structures in PN-CD-200 from the synthesis process (Fig. 2d). Although 6-AQ is composed of only carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen, the elemental analysis results (Table S2) demonstrate the substantial involvement of oxygen in PN-CD-200 (up to 19.33%) during the reaction.

The optical properties and electronic band structure of PN-CD-200 were investigated using UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and FL excitation/emission spectroscopy ( Fig. 3a-c and Fig. S7-9). Fig. 3a and S7 show the UV–visible absorption and FL excitation spectra for PN-CD-200 and 6-AQ. The absorption spectrum for PN-CD-200 in Fig. 3a displayed a primary peak in the range of 270–300 nm, secondary peaks at approximately 400 nm, and tertiary peaks in the range of 420–500 nm. The absorption spectrum of 6-AQ was examined and compared to that of PN-CD-200 to gain further insights into the origin and formation of electronic states in PN-CD-200. For 6-AQ, the initial peak below 300 nm is ascribed to π -π* transitions within the benzenoid ring, whereas the subsequent peak at around 340 nm is associated with n-π* transitions in the pyridinic substructure. This indicates that, in PN-CD-200, the primary absorption peak (270–300 nm) can be attributed to the π-π* transition within the polyaromatic structures of carbonized cores, and the secondary/tertiary peaks are due to interstate (n-π*) transitions that involve pyridinic nitrogen [52,53]. Furthermore, the splitting of the pyridinic absorption peak in PN-CD-200 can arise from the presence of adjacent chemical functional groups (such as hydroxyl or carbonyl groups) and the electronic influence of polyaromatization during carbonization, which can affect the n orbital of pyridinic nitrogen.

The FL excitation spectrum recorded at an emission wavelength of 520 nm exhibits three distinct peaks at excitation wavelengths of ∼320, 390, and 450 nm (Fig. 3a). These peaks can be associated with n orbitals of pyridinic nitrogen (or pyridinic states) within PN-CD-200 [54], where its lone-paired electrons were pumped into π* orbitals of the polyaromatic units, adjacent carbonyl groups, or the pyridine structure itself by excitation light. The FL emission spectra of PN-CD-200 were examined for determining the relationship between their FL characteristics and the presence of pyridinic states. The emission contour map (Fig. 3b and Fig. S8c) and spectra (Fig. 3c and Fig. S9c) exhibit excitation-energy-independent emission peaks consistently centered at 520 nm, observed across excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 nm to 480 nm. Compared to the emission spectrum of 6-AQ (Fig. S8e), the emission band of PN-CD-200 exhibited a red shift of ∼100 nm and significantly broadened photo-excitable range. This suggests that the polyaromatization of the pyridine structure, coupled with oxidation, occurs during the reaction, which reduces the optical band-gap and generates additional oxygenic states that fill the molecular forbidden gap. In contrast to most CDs, PN-CD-200 exhibited an emission bandwidth with an FWHM of ∼70 nm, which is in strict adherence to Kasha's rule [55]. This observation indicates a well-defined electronic structure and optical band-gap, which is attributable to the uniform chemical composition centered around the pyridine units. PN-CD-200 demonstrated high absolute quantum yields of 44.4% and 56.3% under 320 and 470 nm excitation, respectively, because of their exceptional FL emission properties (Fig. S10).

The optical band-gap of PN-CD-200 was determined using a Tauc plot, which reveals a direct band-gap energy of 2.52 eV (Fig. 3d). This finding corroborates the presence of well-defined optically active chemical structures derived from 6-AQ within PN-CD-200 and the attribution of FL emission to these structures. We monitored the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of PN-CD-200 at a probe wavelength of 520 nm for investigating their recombination dynamics (Fig. 3e). Photocarriers were excited to different energy states by employing excitation wavelengths of 320, 380, and 470 nm. Fig. S11 shows that the TRPL decay profiles exhibited consistent time constants (lifetimes) of approximately 5 ns irrespective of excitation wavelengths (Table S3). This observation implies the formation of well-defined pyridine-derived energy states within PN-CD-200, resulting in the predominant occupation of photocarriers in these pyridinic energy states across all excitation wavelengths, firmly adhering to Kasha's rule.

Extended Hückel calculations were performed to investigate the molecular orbital theory descriptions of PN-CD-200. Pyranthrene, a polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon unit commonly formed under the reaction conditions [56], was selected as the base molecule (Fig. 3f). The molecular orbital energy levels and band structures of the four pyranthrene variants containing varying numbers (1−4) of pyridine units were calculated and compared with the experimental data (Fig. 3g and Fig. S12). The energy gap of pyranthrene was 4.91 eV (Fig. 3f), and we simulate the energy gap variations resulting from the incorporation of pyridinic nitrogen into pyranthrene. Fig. 3g and Fig. S12 show that the simulated energy gap decreases from 3.57 to 2.47 eV with an increase in the number of pyridinic nitrogen units in pyranthrene from 1–4. The energy gap of pyranthrene containing four pyridinic nitrogen units aligns well with the optical band-gap energy of PN-CD-200 estimated from the FL emission spectrum and Tauc analysis (2.5 eV). This finding corroborates the experimental results and validates the computational approach. However, the simulation results do not necessarily indicate the existence of a specific structure in our PN-CD-200. Instead, they suggest that pyridinic nitrogen units play a crucial role in generating the optical band-gap responsible for the green FL of PN-CD-200.

Finally, we fabricated LEDs using PN-CD-200 with co-hosts of CBP and TPBi in a 1:1 ratio (by weight) to demonstrate the potential of PN-CDs as a light emitter in self-emissive LEDs. The emitting layer (EML) included PN-CD-200 as a dopant (20 wt% compared to co-host materials). A self-organized polymeric buffer hole-injection layer (Buf-HIL) and TPBi serve as hole-injection/transport and electron transport layers, respectively ( Fig. 4a). The Buf-HIL is composed of PFI and PEDOT:PSS. PFI tends to self-organize on top of PEDOT:PSS during the spin-coating of the Buf-HIL solution on the glass/ITO substrate. This self-organization of the top-rich PFI enhances hole-injection efficiency into the EML and prevents exciton quenching at the Buf-HIL/EML interfaces [57]. Under an applied bias, electrons and holes are injected through TPBi and Buf-HIL, respectively. Subsequently, they are transported to the CBP:TPBi:PN-CD-200 EML, which leads to the emission of electroluminescence (EL) via radiative recombination in the PN-CD-200 dopants. The PN-CD-200 LEDs demonstrate a maximum current efficiency, maximum power efficiency, maximum luminance, and external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 1.17 cd/A, 0.64 lm/W, 300.37 cd/m2, and 0.71% (Fig. 4b-e). The EL spectrum (applied bias = 4.6 V) peaked at 520 nm with a FWHM of ∼80 nm (Fig. 4f and Fig. S13), closely matching the FL spectrum (Fig. 3b and Fig. S8c) and indicating that EL emanated from the PN-CD dopants in the EML. This demonstration suggests that PN-CDs hold potential for LED applications and are promising candidates for future eco-friendly and sustainable display technologies.

Experiments were conducted on PN-CDs prepared at different synthesis temperatures (designated as PN-CD-100, PN-CD-150, PN-CD-200, and PN-CD-300 for the synthesis temperatures of 100, 150, 200, and 300 ℃, respectively) to elucidate the precise mechanism governing PN-CD formation. TEM analysis (Fig. S1) of PN-CD-100, PN-CD-150, and PN-CD-200 revealed a consistent morphology and size; therefore, spherical particles of sizes in the 50–100 nm range were formed across all synthesis temperatures. Intriguingly, no particulate structures were observed for PN-CD-300; instead, it appears as a dense solid carbon film that suggests the complete carbonization and agglomeration of precursor molecules at the elevated temperature of 300 ℃.

Raman analysis (Fig. S2) reveals that D and G bands are not distinctly visible in PN-CD-100, which indicates that 100 ℃ is a relatively low temperature for efficient carbonization. However, PN-CD-150, PN-CD-200, and PN-CD-300 each display two distinct peaks at ∼1360 and 1550 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively. The calculated D/G ratios (0.95–0.97) for different synthesis temperatures (Table S1) suggest minimal change in the internal structure of PN-CDs beyond 100 ℃. Fig. S3 shows that all PN-CDs have XRD peaks in the 2θ range of 17–18°, indicating that they possess an amorphous-like oxidized carbon core regardless of the synthesis temperature. Therefore, the synthesis temperature does not induce substantial changes in carbon morphology or hybridization in PN-CDs, and this is attributed to the dominance of thermal oxidation by air over other reaction effects like intermolecular fusion and polyaromatization.

We conducted FTIR spectroscopy for PN-CDs prepared at different synthesis temperatures to explore the temperature-dependent aspects of their chemical structures (Fig. S4). All PN-CDs exhibited a consistent FTIR pattern regardless of synthesis temperature. However, for PN-CD-300, prominent signals for alcohol O-H stretching (3365 cm−1) and imine CN stretching (1662 cm−1) were observed, whereas the alkene CC bending (840 cm−1) signal was conspicuously absent. These observations suggest that the synthesis temperature influences the chemical composition of PN-CDs, but does not alter carbon morphology or the extent of polyaromatization.

We employed 1H NMR spectroscopic analysis to gain insights into the temperature-induced chemical changes (Fig. S5). A reduction in the intensities of amine and 2-carbon proton signals of 6-AQ at δ = 6.0–7.0 ppm was observed at the lowest synthesis temperature of 100 ℃, along with the emergence of sp2 carbon proton signals at δ = 1.0–2.0 ppm. Pyridine signals at δ = 7.5–9.0 ppm were sustained up to 200 ℃; however, they diminished at 300 ℃, which indicates the oxidation and destruction of pyridine units at synthesis temperatures >200 ℃.

Further, temperature-dependent chemical characteristics can be discerned by XPS analysis (Fig. S6). The oxygen 1 s XPS spectra reveal the presence of a significant amount of oxygen in all PN-CDs, primarily in the form of C−O or CO. The elemental analysis results listed in Table S2 demonstrate a substantial increase in oxygen content in PN-CDs from 3.5% to 19.3% with temperature rising from 100 to 200 ℃, followed by a decrease to 10.36% at 300 ℃. Given the significantly lower reaction yield of PN-CD-300 compared to the other samples, we infer that above 200 ℃, a substantial portion of 6-AQ undergoes oxidation, evaporating in the form of CO2 (Scheme 1). This results in the formation of fully carbonized structures with relatively higher carbon content. This chemical characteristic distinguishes the optical properties of PN-CD-300 from those of other samples. Moreover, the nitrogen 1 s XPS spectra indicate significant pyridine formation in PN-CDs, excluding PN-CD-100. Since the synthesis of PN-CDs involves the nitrogen-containing precursor and thermal oxidation in air, the generation of pyridinic structures is a natural consequence. However, the synthesis temperature of 100 ℃ was insufficient to oxidize the nitrogen-containing functional groups of 6-AQ, resulting in somewhat different optical properties for PN-CD-100 compared to other samples.

The absorption spectra of PN-CD-100, PN-CD-150, and PN-CD-200 consistently feature distinct peaks at 300, 370, and 400 nm. However, PN-CD-300 lacks notable absorption peaks across the entire visible spectrum range (Fig. S7). This observation corresponds with the XPS data of PN-CD-300, signifying substantial carbonization and aggregation at 300 ℃, affording charcoal-like products. This ultimately leads to the absence of the optical band-gap and absorption band observed in other samples.

This trend is evident in the FL emission spectra (Fig. S8 and Fig. S9). The FL emission spectra of PN-CD-100, PN-CD-150, and PN-CD-200 consistently exhibit a distinct peak at 520 nm (Fig. S8a-c); however, PN-CD-300 lacks notable emission peaks in this range (Fig. S8d). These results confirm that excessive carbonization of 6-AQ occurred excessively at elevated synthesis temperatures (>200 ℃), leading to the destruction of chemical structures essential for generating appropriate electronic configurations that govern optical properties such as absorption and emission. PN-CD-100 displayed an additional emission peak around 410 nm (Fig. S8a). Considering that 6-AQ exhibits an emission peak at the same wavelength (Fig. S8e), we deduced that PN-CD-100 maintains the molecular nature of the precursor. This is attributed to the relatively low synthesis temperature of 100 ℃.

From our findings we infer that the synthesis temperature does not substantially influence the morphology of PN-CDs; however, it does bring about changes in chemical compositions and structures. Thus, a moderate carbonization of 6-AQ at an optimal synthesis temperature is crucial for forming pyridine-incorporated core structures that generate the optical band-gap for absorption and emission in the visible spectrum range. Moreover, fine-tuning the synthesis temperatures would govern the extent of oxidation and carbonization of 6-AQ, thereby refining the chemical and electronic structures of the resulting PN-CDs and narrowing the fluorescence emission band to a singular peak.

We investigated the synthesis of CDs using additional six AQ isomers (2-, 3-, 4-, 5-, 7-, and 8-AQ) to further elucidate the role of the amine group in PN-CD formation, with the reaction temperature maintained at 200 ℃. The amine position in 2-, 3-, and 4-AQ was ineffective in promoting the successive reactions of benzenoid ring destruction and nucleophilic carboxylation, which are essential to form a conjugated carbon network with pyridine units. This lack of reactivity led to the absence of notable PN-CD-like FL around 500 nm (Fig. S14a-c). In contrast, the amine position in 5-, 7-, and 8-AQ facilitated benzenoid ring destruction and nucleophilic carboxylation, forming a pyridine-containing conjugated carbon network, which is a structural motif responsible for the FL around 500 nm observed in PN-CDs. This observation highlights the importance of the presence and position of amines in the formation of optically active heteroatomic structures embedded in the carbon network. However, the emission profiles of 5-, 7-, and 8-AQ-derived CDs exhibited notable degradation and broadening compared to those of the representative 6-AQ-derived CDs or PN-CD-200 (Fig. S14d-f). Thus, the reactivity of 5-, 7-, and 8-AQ was lower than that of 6-AQ, which can be attributed to the difference in the resonance stabilization of the negative charge generated during nucleophilic addition. The resonance stabilization of the negative charge in 6-AQ is more effective than in the other isomers, forming a more stable and active conjugated carbon network. This, in turn, contributes to the more intense emission profile observed in PN-CDs.

We developed an additive-free, solid-state carbonization approach for synthesizing PN-CDs from 6-AQ. Our findings revealed that thermal oxidation and the subsequent carboxyl- and amine-mediated intermolecular fusion of AQ during carbonization lead to the formation of optically active pyridine-centered heteroatomic structures embedded within the carbon network. The proposed synthesis method guaranteed the formation of homogeneous pyridinic emission states, which resulted in excitation-energy-independent green (520 nm) FL with a 70 nm FWHM. We employed a combination of theoretical and experimental methods to unravel the underlying mechanism of FL emission, e.g., time-resolved spectroscopy and extended Hückel calculations. These investigations provided comprehensive insights into the electronic structure and behavior of pyridinic structures and their role in FL emission. Moreover, a comparative study was conducted by varying synthesis temperatures and using six AQ isomers. The results demonstrated that thermal oxidation and benzenoid ring destruction of AQ are critical factors in pyridinic structure formation and FL generation in PN-CDs. This understanding can contribute to the formulation of design principles for optimizing the optical properties of PN-CDs. Further, we successfully fabricated green (520 nm) LEDs with an EQE of 0.71% using these PN-CDs, thereby confirming their potential for future display applications. Thus, our research can pave a way for the development of novel optical materials with enhanced performance and reduced environmental impact.

Yerim Byun: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Dajin Lee: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Soohyun Cho: Visualization, Formal analysis. Su Hwan Lee: Methodology, Investigation. Sang Woo Bae: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Sungyeon Won: Methodology, Conceptualization. Woosung Kwon: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yoonsang Park: Data curation, Conceptualization, Investigation. Young-Hoon Kim: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Young-Hoon Kim and Woosung Kwon report financial support was provided by National Research Foundation of Korea. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Basic Science Programs (NRF-2022R1A2C4002403 and NRF-2022R1C1C1008282) funded by the National Research Foundation of Korea.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2024.174435.
